bins.name: Prospective application and streamlined studies
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports major reforms to streamline large load interconnections but insists new rules apply prospectively only to avoid disrupting in-progress projects. The filer advocates fast-track processing for ready projects, AI-driven and advanced modeling tools to automate studies, standardized milestones within each ISO/RTO, and mandatory data reporting on interconnection outcomes. These changes target elimination of delays and administrative burdens while maintaining engineering rigor.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports reforms that, done right, will help the United States connect new AI and advanced manufacturing investment quickly, while strengthening grid reliability and affordability for all customers.

Amazon Energy respectfully urges the Commission to apply any new rules or policy changes adopted in this proceeding prospectively, and not to large load interconnection requests currently in progress under existing interconnection procedures.

Large load developers have expended significant time and resources developing new large load projects (e.g., completing feasibility and system impact studies, securing sites, permits, and long-lead equipment, and executing definitive agreements) and made substantial investments in reliance on existing interconnection rules and procedures.

Abruptly subjecting these in-progress projects to new requirements or study processes could upend their economics, cause substantial delay, or even force withdrawals, thereby undermining the goals of timely and orderly interconnection that the ANOPR seeks to advance.

Amazon Energy recommends that any rule adopted by the Commission governing the interconnection of large loads include mechanisms like those in Order No. 2023 to prioritize projects that are ready for interconnection.

Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to include in any rule a "fast track" or priority processing track for large load projects that can demonstrate a high degree of readiness (e.g., projects that have (i) committed customers, (ii) secured all necessary permits, (iii) site control, (iv) secured financing or corporate commitments, and/or (v) an executed service agreement).
Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to leverage technology and process innovations to automate and accelerate large load interconnections.

The Commission should incentivize or require transmission providers to deploy modern software tools, such as advanced grid modeling and AI-driven analysis, that can conduct feasibility and system impact studies more expeditiously and efficiently.

Amazon Energy emphasizes that greater automation is not a substitute for sound engineering judgment, but a way to expedite engineers' and planners' work by handling administratively burdensome tasks and analyses in hours or days rather than weeks or months.

Amazon Energy urges the Commission to consider imposing similar standardization and requirements here to ensure the timely and efficient processing of large load interconnection requests, particularly for interconnections within the same ISO/RTO.

Improving the caliber of projects at entry will allow the processing of large load interconnections to function more efficiently, with fewer late-stage withdrawals and cascading adverse effects for later large load interconnection requests.

standardizing milestones and requirements for large load interconnections within a common ISO/RTO will lead to better transmission planning outcomes, and in turn, result in improved accuracy in load forecasting.

Amazon Energy recommends that the Commission direct transmission providers to report non-confidential, aggregated data on the processing of large load interconnection request outcomes, such as withdrawal rates, success rates, and processing times.

This information will allow for (i) more accurate transmission system and large load planning and development, and (ii) regulators and market participants to make more informed decisions regarding resource adequacy, load growth, and reliability.

Improving the quality of large load interconnection requests through stricter criteria and milestones and enhanced visibility into the processing of large load interconnections through standardized reporting will result in market efficiencies, shorter development timelines, and lower costs for consumers.

bins.name: Standardized deposits and cost efficiency
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports applying generator-style standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties to large loads to deter speculative projects and reduce restudies. These measures improve project quality at entry, reduce late-stage withdrawals and cascading delays, and ultimately yield lower costs for consumers and more accurate transmission planning.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports the proposal in the ANOPR that, "like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties,"

requiring large load interconnection customers to make upfront study deposits, demonstrate a degree of project readiness (e.g., site control or an executed facilities construction contract), and face withdrawal penalties if they exit after certain milestones will discourage non-viable or speculative large load requests.

Improving the quality of large load interconnection requests through stricter criteria and milestones and enhanced visibility into the processing of large load interconnections through standardized reporting will result in market efficiencies, shorter development timelines, and lower costs for consumers.

bins.name: Co-location and hybrid facility pathways
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports co-location of large loads with generation facilities and hybrid interconnection models. The filer views coordinated load and generation interconnection as beneficial to ratepayers, reduces administrative burden on utilities and RTOs, and enables flexibility for customers. Regulatory clarity on co-location viability is needed urgently.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports co-location of large load projects.

Amazon Energy also recognizes there may be benefits for all ratepayers to interconnecting large loads directly to the bulk electric system in coordination with the interconnection of generation facilities.

creating viable pathways to allow large load customers and generators to interconnect to the bulk electric system, and to study, model, and operate with some form of interrelationship would yield benefits for the bulk electric system as a whole, including reduced administration for utilities, RTOs/ISOs, and investors.
Amazon Energy supports solutions that provide the most flexibility to meet the needs of customers and businesses, which can include co-location.

Amazon Energy respectfully encourages the Commission to expeditiously issue an order in the pending proceeding in Docket No. AD24-11-000, which would provide the industry with much needed guidance as to the viability of large loads co-locating at generating facilities going forward.

bins.name: Standardized study deposits and readiness requirements
bins.desc: Amazon Energy endorses standardized upfront study deposits and readiness criteria (site control, permits, financing commitments, executed agreements) for large loads, mirroring generator interconnection rules. This approach filters non-viable and speculative projects early, reduces queue backlog, and improves interconnection processing efficiency.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports the proposal in the ANOPR that, "like generating facilities, load and hybrid facilities should be subject to standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties,"

requiring large load interconnection customers to make upfront study deposits, demonstrate a degree of project readiness (e.g., site control or an executed facilities construction contract), and face withdrawal penalties if they exit after certain milestones will discourage non-viable or speculative large load requests.

bins.name: Hybrid rights and co-location flexibility
bins.desc: Amazon Energy supports flexible interconnection rights for hybrid facilities (loads operating with generation at a common point) and advocates for regulatory pathways enabling loads and generators to coordinate study, modeling, and operations at the same interconnection point.
quotes: Amazon Energy supports co-location of large load projects.

Amazon Energy also recognizes there may be benefits for all ratepayers to interconnecting large loads directly to the bulk electric system in coordination with the interconnection of generation facilities.

creating viable pathways to allow large load customers and generators to interconnect to the bulk electric system, and to study, model, and operate with some form of interrelationship would yield benefits for the bulk electric system as a whole, including reduced administration for utilities, RTOs/ISOs, and investors.
Amazon Energy supports solutions that provide the most flexibility to meet the needs of customers and businesses, which can include co-location.

Amazon Energy respectfully encourages the Commission to expeditiously issue an order in the pending proceeding in Docket No. AD24-11-000, which would provide the industry with much needed guidance as to the viability of large loads co-locating at generating facilities going forward.

bins.name: Readiness criteria for fast-track qualification
bins.desc: Amazon Energy proposes specific readiness criteria for fast-track priority processing: committed customers, all necessary permits secured, site control, secured financing or corporate commitments, and executed service agreements. These criteria ensure only commercially viable projects receive expedited review.
quotes: Amazon Energy encourages the Commission to include in any rule a "fast track" or priority processing track for large load projects that can demonstrate a high degree of readiness (e.g., projects that have (i) committed customers, (ii) secured all necessary permits, (iii) site control, (iv) secured financing or corporate commitments, and/or (v) an executed service agreement).
